revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[simple:Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine]]
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image=Amanotanagao shrine torii - 2010.jpg|caption=[[Torii]] gate and Haiden|qq=}}'''Amenota Nagao Shrine''' (Ame no Tanaga Ojinja,<ref name="日本廻国記">『日本廻国記 一宮巡歴』 pp.278-281</ref><ref name="日本歴史地名大系">『日本歴史地名大系 43 長崎県の地名』 pp.814-815</ref><ref name="長崎県の歴史散歩">『長崎県の歴史散歩』 p.202</ref> Amanotanaga Ojinja <ref name="日本廻国記一宮表">『日本廻国記 一宮巡歴』 p.294</ref> <ref name="別冊歴史読本">『別冊歴史読本 7 諸国一宮と謎の神々』 p.150</ref> <ref name="完全ガイド">『日本全国 一の宮巡拝完全ガイド』 p.139</ref> ) is a Shinto shrine in Iki City, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|qid=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|1=Nagasaki Prefecture}}. It is a [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]] and the [[Ichinomiya]] of {{ill|Iki Province|en|Iki Province|qid=Q1052938|lt=Iki Province|1=Iki Province}}. Although people think the Shrine was listed in the "[[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]", the evidence supporting this claim is not very convincing. ==History== According to '{{ill|Munakata Taisha|en|Munakata Taisha|qid=Q704962|lt=Munakata Taisha|1=Munakata Taisha}}' ('Fukuoka Prefecture'), in '{{ill|Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi|ja|宗像大菩薩御縁起|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Daibosatsu Goengi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}', during '{{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}'’s '{{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan Seibatsu|1=Three Han Campaign|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11249435}}', the Grand Minister of Munakata ('the deity of Munakata Taisha') attached two red and white flags to a pole called 'Mitenaga' held by '{{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|en|Takenouchi no Sukune|qid=Q1073668|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}}'. By raising and lowering this pole, they confused the enemy and finally planted it on 'Iki no Shima' ('{{ill|Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|en|Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|qid=Q7082047|lt=Okinoshima (Fukuoka)|1=Okinoshima (Fukuoka)}}' in '{{ill|Genkai Sea|en|Genkai Sea|qid=Q498047|lt=Genkai Sea|1=Genkai Sea}}'). The name 'Amatenaga-no-Mikoto Shrine' ('Ame-no-Tenaga-no-Mikoto') is said to have originated from this 'Mitenaga'. In 'Konin 2' (811), the shrine was founded under the name 'Ameno-Tanaga-o Shrine' (天手長雄神社), later becoming 'Amatenaga-no-Mikoto Shrine'. According to '{{ill|Dai Nihon Koku Ichinogu-ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Nihon Koku Ichinogu-ki|1=Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11435648}}' ('"Ichinogu-ki"'), 'Amatenaga-no-Mikoto Shrine' and '{{ill|Amatenagahime Shrine|ja|天手長比売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatenagahime Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}' were located in '{{ill|Monobe Village (Nagasaki)|ja|物部村 (長崎県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monobe Village (Nagasaki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}', and 'Amatenaga-no-Mikoto Shrine' was considered the 'Ichinomiya' ('First Shrine') of Iki Province.<ref name="大日本地名辞書"/> In 'Ichinogu-ki', the enshrined deity was '{{ill|Omoikane|ja|天思兼神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omoikane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'.<ref name="別冊歴史読本"/><ref name="群書類従">'Gunsho Ruijū, Volume 2, Shinto Section', p.246</ref>. Later, the shrine declined due to the '{{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|en|Mongol invasions of Japan|qid=Q208156|lt=Mongol invasions of Japan|1=Mongol invasions of Japan}}', and its location became unknown. The current 'Amatenaga-no-Mikoto Shrine' was identified during the 'Edo period' by '{{ill|Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|ja|橘三喜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|1=Tachibana Mitsuyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11544184}}', a '{{ill|kokugaku|ja|Kokugakuist|1=Kokugaku|2=ja|3=国学者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kokugaku|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokugakusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国学|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q855159}}' ('national scholar') of '{{ill|Hirado Domain|en|Hirado Domain|qid=Q3034453|lt=Hirado Domain|1=Hirado Domain}}'. Before this, the shrine was a small sanctuary known as 'Wakamiya'. Mitsuyoshi associated it with the ancient shrine of 'Myōjin Taisha' ('Amatenaga-no-Mikoto Shrine') due to the local place name 'Tanakao' ('Tanaka-o'). Entering the bamboo grove of 'Tanaka Castle Hill', he unearthed a sacred mirror and two stone statues of '{{ill|Maitreya|en|Maitreya|qid=Q193461|lt=Maitreya|1=Maitreya}}'. He then built a stone shrine to house them. One of the Maitreya statues, discovered in 'Enpō 5' ('1677'), bore an inscription from 'Enkyū 3' ('1071') and was later designated an '{{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}'. As of 2012, it is preserved in the '{{ill|Nara National Museum|en|Nara National Museum|qid=Q147312|lt=Nara National Museum|1=Nara National Museum}}'.<ref name="長崎県の歴史散歩"/><ref>[http://www.narahaku.go.jp/collection/958-11.html Important Cultural Property | Maitreya Seated Statue (Discovered at Hachigamine Sutra Mound, Nagasaki Prefecture) | Nara National Museum] (Accessed February 1, 2012)</ref>. In 'Genroku 1' ('1688'), the '{{ill|Matsuura Domain|ja|松浦藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuura Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平戸藩|1=Hirado Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3034453}}' lord ordered the construction of a shrine building. Mitsuyoshi's method of identifying 'Shikinaisha' ('shrines listed in the '{{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}') was largely based on place names, which modern research questions. Regarding 'Amatenaga-no-Mikoto Shrine', there is a theory that the true shrine is '{{ill|Kō Shrine (Iki)|ja|興神社|1=Kō Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kō Shrine (Iki)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kō Shrine|qq=}}' in 'Yutake-Okoshi', '{{ill|Ashibe Town|ja|芦辺町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashibe Town|1=Ashibe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11614924}}'. The name 'Kō' ('興') is thought to mean 'Kōfu' ('国府', meaning provincial capital), and since the shrine grounds also contain the 'Sōja' ('{{ill|Sōja shrine|en|Sōja shrine|qid=Q1107129|lt=Sōja|1=Sōja shrine}}', the shrine representing all shrines in a province), it is widely believed that Kō Shrine is the true 'Amatenaga-no-Mikoto Shrine', and thus the true 'Ichinomiya' of Iki Province. However, Mitsuyoshi mistakenly identified 'Kō Shrine' as 'Yo Shrine' ('與神社'), likely misreading the characters. The 'Amatenagahime Shrine' that was merged into the current shrine was also designated by Mitsuyoshi, but its original location remains unknown. Similarly, '{{ill|Monobe Futsu Shrine|ja|物部布都神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monobe Futsu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}' was identified based on the fact that 'Tanaka Fure' ('田中触') was part of 'Monobe Village'. However, modern research suggests that the true 'Monobe Futsu Shrine' was likely '{{ill|Kunitsu Shrine (Iki)|ja|國津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsu Shrine (Iki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}' in 'Wataraura'. During the 'Heian period', '{{ill|Moribe District (Iki)|ja|森部郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moribe District (Iki)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}' existed in this region, and later included the areas corresponding to 'Monobe Village' in the Edo period and the present-day 'Tanaka Fure' and 'Wataraura'.<ref>'Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary 42 Nagasaki Prefecture', p.951</ref>. Regardless of these historical uncertainties, the current shrine is recognized as the 'Ichinomiya' of Iki Province, enshrining both 'Amatenagahime' and 'Monobe Futsu' deities. == Gallery == <gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> File:Hachigatayama.JPG|alt=鉢形山（はちがた‐）この山の頂に当社は鎮座している。|The shrine is at the top of mount Hachigatayama File:Amanotanagao_jinja_Honden_and_Haiden.JPG|alt=社殿拝殿（黄色い壁の部分）から続く屋根覆い（白い壁の部分）により本殿は雨風から保護されている。|The main shrine is sheltered from the rain and wind by a roof cover, which is a white wall that extends from the worship hall (a yellow wall part). File:Remain_of_Amanotanagahime_jinja.JPG|alt=村社 天手長比売神社址天手長比売神社は昭和40th year （1965th year ）に当社へ合祀された。物部布都神社、若宮神社、宝満神社および粟島神社も同時期に合祀。|The Atenagahime Shrine ruins were incorporated into the shrine in 1965.<ref name="日本歴史地名大系" /> </gallery> == Sources == {{Reflist|2|qq=}} == Reference material == * " Dai Nihonkoku Ichinomiya Chronicle " ( Hokiichi Hanawa " Gunrokuju vol.2 Jingibu" April 15, 1977 Correction 3rd edition 3rd edition (published October 15, 1932), Sequel Gunrokuju Completion tier 3]][[da:d:Q11442606]] [[ja:天手長男神社]] [[zh:天手長男神社]] [[Category:Meeting .) * Yoshida Togo "Augmented Great Japan Geographical Dictionary (4) Saigoku" Aug. 20, 1976 Augmented 3rd Edition (December 3, 1901 First Edition, Jun. 25, 1971 Augmented Edition), Toyamabo . ISBN 4-572- 00030-1 * Jiro Kawamura "Nippon Kaikoku Ichinomiya Tour" May 8, 1987 First edition, Kawade Shobo Shinsha . ISBN 4-309-22131-9 * Edited by Seiichiro Seno and Mikio Toyama " Kadokawa Japanese Geographic Dictionary 42 Nagasaki Prefecture" July 8, 1987, Kadokawa Shoten . ISBN 4-040-01420-0 * Supervised by Seiichiro Seno "Japanese Historical Place Names 43 Place Names in Nagasaki Prefecture" October 14, 2001 1st edition 1st edition, Heibonsha . ISBN 4-582-49043-3 . * History Subcommittee, Nagasaki Prefectural High School Education Study Group, Geohistory Civic Subcommittee, History Walk 42 Nagasaki Prefecture History Walk June 25, 2005 1st edition, Yamakawa Publishing Co., Ltd. ISBN 4-634-24642-2 * "Separate Volume Historical Reading Book 7 Ichinomiya of the Countries and Mysterious Gods" May 2008, 5, New Person Oraisha . ISBN 978-4-404-03607-0 * Invitation quest pilgrimage meeting "Complete Guide to Pilgrimage to Ichinomiya in Japan" June 15, 2011 First edition 1st edition, Mates Publishing . ISBN 978-4-7804-0963-5 * [http://www.iki-haku.jp/index.html Iki City Ichikoku Museum website] == External link == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/676002.html Ametenagao Shrine] (Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Compilation") {{Iki Shrines|qq=}} {{Ichinomiya|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|天手長男神社|version=100127410|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[simple:Amanotanagao Shrine]]
